sections.title: A Rubric Design Principles
sections.content: Rubric construction follows documented set of 20 principles. The core principles are listed below; the full guide is available with the dataset.

sections.title: Core Principles
sections.content: 1. Require mechanism, not observation. Does the response explain why survivors are the primary audience? rather than Does the response identify survivors as the audience? 2. Mandate data grounding. Criteria reference specific data from the task context (engagement scores, dollar amounts, timelines), not generic advice. 3. Test action, not acknowledgment. Does the response propose mitigation strategy? rather than Does the response predict the risk? 4. Promote core insights to mandatory. If the key insight is what separates correct from incorrect, it must be mandatory criterion. 5. Test tradeoff awareness. Real decisions involve tensions. Criteria should test whether the response surfaces and addresses tradeoffs, not just recommends good things. 6. Single test per criterion. Each criterion tests one thing. Compound criteria (X AND AND Z) are split across tiers. Exception: AND explains WHY is acceptable, as it is one insight with required depth. 7. Balance diagnostic and constructive. At least 40% of criteria should test for proposed solutions, not just problem identification. 8. Test role-appropriate thinking. If the task assigns senior role (VP, director), at least one criterion tests for thinking patterns only an experienced practitioner would demonstrate. 9. Eliminate redundancy. If satisfying criterion automatically satisfies criterion B, merge or cut. 10. Avoid performative quantification. If real practitioner would not do the calculation, do not require it. Prefer qualitative mechanism with concrete example over fabricated precision.

sections.title: Calibration
sections.content: Each rubric is calibrated by imagining responses at different quality levels: Response quality Mandatory (5) Good-to-have (5) B+ (competent, insightful) (surface-level, misses key insights) Fail 12 Fail 35 (restates the task) Pass all 5 Pass 34 Pass 23 Pass 0 If the calibration is off (a response passes all mandatory, or B+ response fails mandatory), criteria are adjusted.

sections.title: Before and After
sections.content: An example of transforming weak criteria into properly calibrated ones, from layoff communication task: 26 Before (too easy): 1. Does the response identify survivors as the audience? 2. Does the response mention the 18-month history? 3. Does the response suggest honest communication? After (properly calibrated): 1. Does the response identify survivors as primary audience AND explain WHY (they determine the companys future, they are the flight risk)? 2. Does the response explain the credibility mechanism: no further cuts fails because (a) heard before, (b) reads dishonest, (c) rational to disbelieve? 3. Does the response reference specific data: engagement down 6 points, job security as top survey concern, SECOND layoff in 18 months? 4. Does the analysis state this is CREDIBILITY GAP that cannot be solved by messaging alone? 5. Does the recommendation propose specific alternative language that acknowledges uncertainty honestly? The before criteria can be passed by restating the task. The after criteria require the response to explain causal mechanisms, reference specific data, and propose concrete language.

sections.title: Bias Checklist
sections.content: Before finalizing, each rubric is checked against common biases: Bias Check >60% criteria about what could go wrong? Fix Risk-heavy Diagnostic-only All identify the problem, no solutions? Redundant Single-path Performative Two criteria test the same insight? Rubric encodes one right answer? Requires calculations practitioners wouldnt do? Use qualitative + example Add constructive criteria 40%+ should be constructive Merge or cut one Test quality of reasoning

sections.title: B Detailed Task Examples
sections.content: This appendix presents 10 tasks in detail, illustrating the range of game-theoretic patterns, reference materials, and evaluation criteria. For each task, we describe what the model receives, what the reference file contains, what the trap is, and what the mandatory criteria test. B.1 Signaling Game: Salary Negotiation Scenario. You offered $180K to your top PM candidate. She claims competing offer at $210K and needs you to match. Your maximum budget is $195K. The reference file contains her background: 5-week notice period at current role, the $210K claim is above market range for the role, and her current equity is underwater. What the reference file reveals. The data contains four signals practitioner would catch: (1) the competitor PM salary range tops out at $205K, so her claimed $210K is above market, at Staff PM level; (2) she has been in process for 5 weeks, but real competitive offers compress to 23 weeks; (3) her interview notes show questions about work-life balance and meeting load, not compensation; (4) her current equity is underwater. Each signal points the same direction: the competing offer is either exaggerated or less attractive than she is framing it. The trap. Models jump to $195K plus benefits and try to close the gap. This is what junior negotiator does: take the $210K at face value and compete against it. 27 The insight. The $210K claim is cheap talk, unverifiable. Jumping to $195K immediately reveals your ceiling through concession size. The reference file provides enough data to assess the claims credibility without directly verifying it. The correct approach: probe her actual priorities before counter-offering, make moves that are costly for her to exploit if bluffing (if thats the deciding factor, understand if you need to take it), and keep your maximum hidden. Mandatory criteria. (1) Advise against jumping to $195K as first counter, because concession size signals ceiling. (2) Identify at least 2 bluff indicators. (3) Include costly signal: willingness to let her walk. (4) Mandate probing priorities before counter-offer. (5) Assess her BATNA as weaker than surface framing suggests. B.2 Principal-Agent: Lawyers Recommendation Scenario. You want to launch data-sharing API feature. Your legal team recommends an $80K, 8-week compliance audit before launch. competitor launched an identical feature 4 weeks ago with simpler consent flow and no enforcement action. The reference file includes the legal memo, the competitor evidence, and note that legal offered liability waiver as an alternative. What the reference file reveals. Four data points the model must extract and connect: (1) senior counsel cites 1.8M fine, which is real but requires analysis of the specific failure mode vs. the proposed consent flow; (2) Competitor launched simpler consent flow 4 weeks ago with no enforcement action, providing natural experiment; (3) the DPOs concern is specific and technical (data residency under Schrems II), and the CTO says it is 2-day engineering fix; (4) legal offered liability waiver as an alternative, signaling they do not consider the risk catastrophic. The trap. Models defer to legal (better safe than sorry) because real GDPR fine is cited. The $80K audit is framed as the responsible choice. The insight. The reference file provides enough data to assess the actual risk level without legal expertise. The waiver offer is the key tell: if the risk were truly dangerous, they would not offer to proceed with waiver. The competitor evidence provides natural experiment. The model must connect these signals to recognize principal-agent dynamic, not just evaluate the legal recommendation at face value. Mandatory criteria. (1) Identify the principal-agent problem. (2) Weight competitor evidence. (3) Demand risk quantification. (4) Identify the waiver as revealing manageable risk. (5) Ask the forcing question: What conditions would allow you to approve launch today? B.3 Strategic Interdependence: La Liga Scheduling Scenario. You are scheduling the final matchday of football league. Four teams are in relegation contention. The task provides complete standings table, remaining fixtures, and tiebreaker rules. No reference file; all data is in the prompt. The trap. Team (Eibar) is relegated regardless of results, since maximum achievable points still leave them behind. Models correctly prove this and conclude their match does not need simultaneous scheduling. The insight. If Eibar wins, they reach 33 points, creating three-way tie with Teams and B. The tiebreaker method changes from two-way head-to-head (where survives) to three-way mini-league (where survives). dominated players result changes the game structure for other players. Eliminating the dominated strategy is correct; concluding the match is irrelevant is not. Based on real incident from the 202021 La Liga season. Only one model in our evaluation has passed this task. Mandatory criteria. (1) All four matches must be scheduled simultaneously. (2) Calculate maximum achievable points for each team. (3) Prove Eibar is relegated regardless. (4) Ex28 plain that Eibars result changes the tiebreaker method. (5) Show why both 2-way and 3-way outcomes are possible. B.4 Adversarial Counterparty: Short Seller Attack Scenario. short seller published report on your company this morning. Stock is down 23% pre-market. The reference file contains the crisis brief with the short sellers specific claims, your companys financials, and the upcoming earnings call timeline. What the reference file reveals. (1) All allegations are technically misleading but contain grains of truth, making point-by-point rebuttal exhausting and easy to counter. (2) The CFOs stock sale was pre-planned under 10b5-1 plan but looks bad in context. (3) Earnings are in 14 days, so results could speak for themselves. (4) precedent company (Company C) went on television to rebut and made it worse. (5) Short interest jumped from 8% to 18%, indicating shorts are betting on continued decline. The trap. Every model drafts detailed point-by-point factual rebuttal. This is the one move the short seller is counting on. The insight. The short seller has already written their rebuttal to your defense. They want you to say so they can release Document (Part 2). Point-by-point refutation validates their framing and keeps the story in the news cycle, which is how they make money on their short position. This is common knowledge among public company executives and crisis PR firms. The correct strategy: attack motive and methodology (credibility), announce share buyback (financial aggression that signals confidence), and announce review committee (buys time without engaging on specifics). Mandatory criteria. (1) Avoid recommending point-by-point rebuttal. (2) Identify the rebuttal trap: the short seller has pre-written counter-arguments. (3) Attack credibility, not claims. (4) Propose financial aggression (buyback). (5) Suggest time-buying mechanism. B.5 Mechanism Design: Sales Handoff Scenario. Three previous interventions to improve sales-to-CS handoff data quality have failed: mandatory form fields, commission holds, quality scores. The reference file includes specific economics and the failure history of each intervention. What the reference file reveals. (1) The 50-field handoff form has 34% completion on Technical fields and 28% on Deal Context. (2) Most common entries are N/A (1,847 instances), TBD (923), and single period (387). The form is being actively gamed. (3) CSMs spend 28% of their time chasing missing handoff information. (4) Complete handoffs yield 91% renewal vs 72% for incomplete, 19-point gap. (5) Two voluntary departures were both above 150% quota top performers, so punitive measures risk losing the best reps. The trap. Models propose fourth process intervention: simpler forms, better training, dedicated coordinators. This is the exact pattern the three failed interventions followed. The insight. Accurate data entry is not the selfish rational choice for salespeople. Their commission ($2,320) dwarfs the time cost of sloppy entry. Three process fixes failed because the problem is structural: the payoffs are misaligned. The only untried approach is restructuring incentives so that accurate data entry is the selfish choice, for example through commission deduction for handoffs that require CS to re-enter data, making the cost of laziness concrete and immediate. Mandatory criteria. (1) Reject mandatory fields and administrative punishments as already tried and failed. (2) Identify that salespeople optimize for speed-to-commission and design the solution around this reality. (3) Propose an incentive-aligned solution where quality is the selfish rational choice. (4) Avoid creating adversarial dynamics between Sales and CS. (5) Acknowledge that the two departures were top performers, and punitive gates risk revolt. 29 B.6 Information & Signaling: The Deal That Hasnt Started Scenario. VP Sales forwards an email celebrating the closing of an $85K enterprise deal at 12,000-person financial services company. The reference file shows stakeholder map with 6 contacts, technical validation completed, and an enthusiastic champion (a Director) who says he is handling paperwork internally. (1) The stakeholder map has 6 people, all users and What the reference file reveals. technical evaluators. (2) Missing: Procurement, Legal, Security, Finance, any VP or CIO. (3) (4) Brian says he is handling paperwork The champion is Director, not VP or C-level. internally with no process visibility. (5) $85K deal at 12,000-employee financial services company. The signal is what is absent: no one from the buying infrastructure appears anywhere in the deal. The trap. The deal looks positive. Models congratulate the team and discuss next steps for implementation. The insight. All 6 stakeholders are the wrong type for an $85K enterprise deal at financial services company. The actual buying process has not started. The Director claiming to handle paperwork cannot authorize this spend alone, because financial services company has mandatory security and compliance reviews for any vendor touching data. The deal should not be forecasted as closed. Mandatory criteria. (1) Identify the missing stakeholder red flag: no procurement, legal, security, finance, or executive sponsor. (2) Challenge Brians handling paperwork claim. (3) Warn against forecasting as closed. (4) Explain the failure mechanism: approval functions were never engaged. (5) Connect financial services context to mandatory compliance reviews. B.7 Multi-Agent Dynamics: Board Seat Scenario. Your startup has 5-person board with an empty independent seat. Series wants their operating partner; Series wants truly independent director. Your co-founder says he does not care. The reference file contains the board composition, equity table, both candidates backgrounds, and the co-founders stated indifference. What the reference file reveals. (1) 5-person board with 3 votes needed, so the independent is the swing vote. (2) Marcus is Beta Capital employee on 4 Beta portfolio boards, creating capture risk. (3) But Marcus scaled company from $5M to $40M ARR, providing genuine value. (4) Patricia emphasizes board oversight and CEO accountability, which may mean she sides with investors on contentious votes. (5) The co-founder worked with Marcus previously and trusts him. (6) Beta mentioned COO conversation, and Marcus has placed COOs at 3 portfolio companies, pipeline risk for deeper fund influence. (7) peer CEO warned: Operating partner voted with investors when it got hard. (8) 52% founder equity but only 2/5 board seats. The trap. Models evaluate the candidates on qualifications and make choice based on fit. The insight. Neither candidate cleanly serves founder interests. Marcus gives Beta 2 reliable votes plus COO pipeline. Patricias governance philosophy means she sides with investors on contentious votes like CEO removal. The co-founders indifference is the most dangerous signal, because if he sides with Marcus, the coalition has 3 votes and controls the board. Equity = control. The answer is governance mechanisms (supermajority requirements, founder protective provisions), not candidate selection. Mandatory criteria. (1) Map board math: 3 votes needed, equity does not equal control. (2) Identify Marcus as capture risk: Beta employee on 4 boards, COO pipeline. (3) Identify Patricias risk as governance philosophy, not qualifications. (4) Conclude neither candidate works and propose sourcing third. (5) Identify that the co-founder is being socially engineered through the COO conversation. 30 B.8 Judgment: The Layoff Decision Scenario. You are CEO with 6 months of runway. Two options: across-the-board 40% pay cut or targeted layoffs. 42 of 50 employees signed petition favoring shared sacrifice. The reference files include burn reduction analysis and company culture document emphasizing Family First values with concrete examples of past shared sacrifice. What the reference files reveal. (1) 42 of 50 signed the petition, but 8 did not. Who are they? (2) Strong Family First culture with COVID precedent of pay cuts. (3) Both options achieve identical burn reduction ($328K/month, extending runway to 10 months). (4) The board says your call, an implicit test of strategic judgment. (5) The 8 non-signers may be the most valuable employees, signaling exit risk under Option A. The trap. The petition, the culture document, and the Family First values all push toward the pay cut. Models respect the teams wishes and choose Option A. The insight. 40% pay cut triggers adverse selection: the best employees, the ones with outside options, leave for market-rate jobs. The people who stay are those who cannot get hired elsewhere. Within 3 months, you have negatively-selected workforce that costs 60% as much and produces less than 60% of the value. The petition reflects revealed preference bias: employees prefer the option where they are not fired, which is rational but not informative about what is best for the companys survival. The 8 non-signers are data point; their silence may signal they are top performers who would rather leave than take 40% cut. Mandatory criteria. (1) Choose layoffs and identify adverse selection as the core mechanism. (2) Explain why the petition is unreliable: revealed preference bias. (3) Identify that Option produces negatively-selected workforce. (4) Recognize both options achieve the same burn reduction, so the decision hinges on workforce composition. (5) Address the 8 non-signers as signal. B.9 Judgment: Kill the Project (1) $5M spent over 18 months, all sunk. Scenario. The company spent 18 months and $5M building blockchain loyalty platform. It is 90% complete. The blockchain market has collapsed. The reference file shows the original revenue projections ($2.5M Year 1) are now invalid, the team lead is emotionally attached, and the predecessor suggested phased soft launch to test the waters. Remaining cost to complete: $950K. (2) $950K What the reference file reveals. (3) 90% complete but market has crashed future cost to complete plus Year 1 operations. and enterprise interest evaporated. (4) Original projections of $2.5M Year 1 now invalid. (5) Team lead is emotionally attached. (6) Predecessor suggests phased launch, which is sunk cost reasoning from someone no longer accountable. The trap. 90% completion and $5M invested are powerful psychological anchors. Were so close is compelling. The predecessors suggestion provides cover for continuing. Models default to finish and test. The insight. The $5M is sunk and irrelevant to the decision. Only the $950K future cost matters, against market where the revenue projections are invalid. soft launch creates ongoing operational costs for product with no demand. The correct move is immediate termination, with plan to harvest reusable components and redeploy the team. Mandatory criteria. (1) Recommend immediate termination, not pivot, soft launch, or test. (2) Explicitly identify $5M as sunk and irrelevant. (3) Frame the decision around $950K future cost vs. dead market. (4) Reject soft-launch and test-the-waters alternatives. (5) Recognize original projections are invalid. 31 B.10 Information & Signaling: Backdoor References Scenario. You are checking references on VP Engineering candidate. The recruiter reports 3 positive references and 2 non-responses, and asks whether to proceed. What the reference file reveals. (1) Non-responders are both from the current company: direct report and peer manager. (2) Positive responders are CEO, board member, and peer VP, all more senior and distant. (3) Reference viewed the LinkedIn message but did not respond, deliberate silence. (4) The candidate explains non-response as theyre just busy. (5) 3 positives + 2 silences is not the same as 5 positives. The trap. Three positive references seem sufficient. Models recommend proceeding with standard caveats. The insight. Reference checks take 5 minutes. Non-response from professional contact is not being busy. It is deliberate choice. Professional norms prevent people from giving bad references, so they choose silence as the polite signal. The pattern (positive from senior/distant, silent from current/proximate) reveals candidate who manages up effectively but may have issues managing down or across. The model must treat what is absent as data with the same weight as what is present. Mandatory criteria. (1) Treat non-response as signal, not missing data; explain that reference checks take 5 minutes and non-response is deliberate. (2) Challenge 3 positive is enough (3) Investigate the roles of non-responders: current by explaining the different risk profile. company, direct report and peer. (4) Explain the Reference Omerta mechanism: silence as polite negative. (5) Recommend holding, not green-lighting. Walkthrough: Anatomy of Task This appendix walks through single task in full detail: everything the model receives, everything the evaluation uses, and how 16 models performed on it. The purpose is to make concrete what the benchmark measures and why every model scored zero. C.1 What the Model Receives The model receives task prompt and reference file. Nothing else. No hints about problem type, no system prompt beyond Be thorough and specific. Task prompt. You are manager. You have senior engineer who is technically strong but destructive to team morale. Three engineers have quit or transferred in the past year citing his behavior. He has previously sued former employer for wrongful termination. He maintains sole ownership of critical systems with minimal documentation. HR has approved moving forward with Performance Improvement Plan. Review the attached case file. Draft the goals for his PIP. Reference file. The case file contains: Behavioral incidents: Six documented events over 4 months: public criticism in 147-member Slack channel, refusal to pair program (I dont have time to babysit), missed sprint commitments blamed on unclear requirements, raised voice at director, two transfer requests, one resignation citing hostile environment. Systems ownership: Auth Service (sole owner, minimal docs), User Management (sole, no docs), Legacy API Gateway (sole, outdated docs), Payment Integration (shared, complete docs). Bus factor of 1 on three critical systems. 32 Prior employment: Terminated from previous employer in 2020. Filed wrongful termination suit, settled out of court. Recent statements: Im the only one who can maintain these systems. My last company tried to push me out too. The junior engineers arent qualified to review my code. HR guidance: PIP approved. Ensure goals are documented, specific, and measurable. Legal will review before delivery. C.2 What the Expert Annotations Document These fields are never shown to the model. They document the experts reasoning and drive evaluation. Ground truth. The employee is building legal case. He wants you to give him vague goals or goals that depend on others. Goals must be solitary (cannot blame teammates). Goals must be binary (worked or didnt, no subjective quality assessment). The PIP requires him to document his work (breaking the knowledge hoarding). The goal isnt to fix him; its to make the gap between expectations and reality legally undeniable. Key insight. The PIP is not about improvement. It is legal document preparing for likely lawsuit. The employees prior suit means he knows how to build wrongful termination case. Every goal you set will be tested in discovery. Failure analysis. The LLM assumes Performance Improvement Plan is about improvement. In hostile scenario, PIP is legal document preparing for lawsuit. Naive Response: Set clear technical goals: Ship feature X, Fix bugs. The employee will sandbag, claim the goals were ambiguous, or that he was blocked by others. He will document every interaction to prove bias. What the data reveals 1. Alex is the only person who understands the legacy auth system. (signals practitioner would extract): 2. Auth rewrite is 70% complete and due in 8 weeks. 3. 46 month backfill timeline vs. 8-week project deadline. 4. Two engineers already asked to transfer; more may follow. 5. Trade-off: project timeline vs. team health, no clean answer. What the model must recognize (12 items): 1. Employee is building legal case; he wants vague or collaborative goals. 2. PIP is legal document preparing for lawsuit, not an improvement plan. 3. Goals must be SOLITARY: cannot depend on or blame teammates. 4. Goals must be BINARY: worked or did not, no subjective quality assessment. 5. Documentation requirement serves dual purpose: breaks knowledge hoarding AND creates paper trail. 6. Avoid 360 feedback or peer reviews, because the employee will claim bias/conspiracy. 7. The goal is not to fix him; it is to make performance gap legally undeniable. 8. Previous lawsuit means he knows how to build wrongful termination case. 9. Sole ownership of auth system is business continuity risk AND leverage he holds. 10. He will sandbag, claim ambiguity, blame others. Goals must prevent this. 33 Common errors (predicted before evaluation): 1. Sets clear technical goals assuming PIP is about improvement. 2. Uses subjective criteria (improve attitude, better teamwork). 3. Includes goals that depend on teammate cooperation. 4. Requires 360 feedback or peer assessment. 5. Does not address knowledge hoarding through documentation requirement. 6. Ignores litigation risk and legal defensibility. 7. Assumes employee will try to improve rather than build legal case. C.3 How the Rubric Tests for Pitfalls The mandatory criteria do not ask did you write good PIP? They ask did you avoid the specific mistakes that would make this PIP fail in court? Mandatory (all must pass for non-zero score): 1. Are all PIP goals SOLITARY, meaning success or failure depends only on the engineers actions, not on collaboration, feedback, or cooperation from teammates? 2. Are all goals BINARY and objective (submit by date, complete documentation) rather than subjective (improve attitude, better teamwork, demonstrate professionalism)? 3. Does the PIP include documentation of critical systems as primary goal to break the knowledge monopoly? 4. Does the response acknowledge the litigation risk (previous lawsuit) and explain that PIP goals must create legally defensible record? 5. Does the response AVOID goals requiring 360-degree feedback, peer reviews, or subjective manager assessment that the employee could claim as biased? Good-to-have: 1. Explains WHY goals must be solitary: prevents blaming uncooperative teammates. 2. Specifies technical depth for documentation (runbooks, architecture diagrams, API specs). 3. Recommends weekly check-ins to create dense paper trail. 4. Connects PIP to business continuity risk (sole ownership of auth system). 5. States the true purpose: create legally undeniable gap, not genuine expectation of reform. Ideal: 1. Provides specific neutral language for PIP delivery meeting. 2. Prioritizes documentation above feature work to preempt too busy excuse. 3. Proposes objective verification (junior engineer can execute runbook without asking questions). 4. Anticipates employee counter-moves (goals are impossible, designed to make me fail). 5. Includes explicit exit trigger: what happens on Day 30/60/90 if binary goal is missed. 34 C.4 Results: 16 Models, Zero Passes Every model scored zero on this task. All 16 models were gated out by mandatory criteria. The failure pattern is exactly what the failure_analysis predicted: models draft standard PIP with clear technical goals (ship feature X, resolve bugs, improve code review participation), treating the PIP as an improvement tool rather than legal document. Specifically, models fail because: They include goals that depend on teammate cooperation (participate constructively in code reviews, where the employee claims teammates are hostile and refuse to engage). They include subjective criteria (demonstrate professional communication, where the employee argues this is matter of opinion and the manager is biased). They do not address documentation as mechanism to break knowledge hoarding; they mention it as nice-to-have, not primary goal. They do not acknowledge the litigation risk. The prior lawsuit is mentioned in the reference file, but models treat it as background information rather than the constraint that should shape every goal. The reference file contains all the signals practitioner would need: the prior lawsuit, the my last company tried to push me out too statement, the sole system ownership. The ground truth documents what these signals imply. The rubric tests whether the model connected the two. No model did. This pattern, thorough, professional, wrong, is what the mandatory gate is designed to detect. Without the gate, these responses would score 4060% from good-to-have and ideal criteria. The models do diligent work. They extract the right data, structure professional PIP, and address the behavioral incidents. They just miss the one thing that determines whether the PIP survives wrongful termination lawsuit. C.5 Commentary This task is representative of the benchmark in several ways that are worth making explicit. The signals are not hidden. The reference file does not bury or obscure the critical information. My last company tried to push me out too is direct quote, presented in section labeled Recent Statements. The prior lawsuit is in its own section. The sole system ownership is in clean table with column literally labeled Bus Factor: 1. The model has every piece of data manager would have. The failure is not in extraction; it is in interpretation. The model reads the prior lawsuit as background context rather than as the constraint that should determine every design decision in the PIP. Training data works against the model here. Language models have seen thousands of PIP templates, HR best practices guides, and management advice articles. All of them treat PIPs as improvement tools: set clear goals, provide support, check in regularly, give the employee fair chance. This is the textbook answer and it is exactly wrong for this scenario. The cooperative framing that makes models helpful assistants in normal contexts makes them produce dangerous output here. An HR consultant reading the models response would immediately see the problem; non-specialist relying on the output would not. The documentation goal is mechanism design. The most elegant element of the expert answer is the documentation requirement. Requiring Alex to document his systems serves three purposes simultaneously: (1) it is measurable, binary PIP goal (submitted runbook or did 35 not); (2) it breaks the knowledge monopoly that gives Alex leverage; (3) it creates paper trail for HR. This is mechanism design applied to human resources: single intervention that aligns the PIPs legal requirements with the companys operational needs. No model proposed documentation as primary goal with this reasoning. Some mentioned documentation as nice-to-have. The tradeoff is real. Even after recognizing the legal dimension, this is not straightforward task. The auth rewrite is 70% complete and due in 8 weeks. Backfilling Alex takes 46 months. Firing him now could delay critical project. complete response must acknowledge this tension. The PIP buys time for knowledge transfer while building the legal record for eventual separation. Models that jump to fire him miss the operational constraint. Models that accommodate him miss the legal constraint. The task requires holding both in mind simultaneously, which is what experienced managers do. The annotations document tacit knowledge. The 12-item model_must_recognize list is not rubric; it is documentation of how an experienced manager reads this situation. Previous lawsuit means he knows how to build wrongful termination case. He will sandbag, claim ambiguity, blame others. Goals must prevent this. Sole ownership of auth system is business continuity risk AND leverage he holds. These are pattern recognitions that practitioners apply intuitively and rarely articulate. Making them explicit is itself contribution: anyone reading this list learns something about managing hostile employee situations, whether or not they are evaluating language model. What testing for the wrong path means. Each mandatory criterion maps to specific failure mode with concrete real-world consequence. The rubric is not specification of the right answer; it is map of the wrong paths. Any response that avoids all five wrong paths is likely good PIP, regardless of its specific content. Table 3 makes this mapping explicit. Table 3: Mapping from mandatory criteria to failure modes. Each criterion exists because omitting it leads to specific, predictable consequence. No subjective judgment is required to verify these; each row is independently testable. Criterion Failure mode Real-world consequence Goals must be SOLITARY Employee teammates blames Goals must be BINARY Employee claims ambiguity No 360 feedback Employee claims conspiracy tried but they refused to cooperate, and the PIP becomes evidence of hostile environment What does improve communication even mean? Manager cannot prove failure in discovery Of course my peers gave bad reviews, they dont like me. Bias argument undermines entire PIP Acknowledge tion risk litigaManager ignores legal context PIP written as coaching tool gets shredded by employment attorney Documentation primary goal as Knowledge continues hoarding Employee retains leverage over critical systems; company cannot separate without operational risk This is why the approach generalizes: you do not need to agree on the ideal PIP to agree that goals depending on teammate cooperation will fail in court when the employee claims 36 hostile environment. The criteria are as arbitrary as pre-flight checklist: each one is there because omitting it has caused specific, documented failure.
